question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Since ContractResult.errorMessage is a plain String with no validation, can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with a crafted errorMessage that falsely indicates a legitimate transaction failed, causing the mirror node to record an unauthorized failure for a contract call that was actually approved?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune(long consensusTimestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the @Transactional annotation on prune() to wrap the deletion in a transaction that also inserts a forged ContractResult, effectively replacing a legitimate authorized record with a malicious one in a single atomic operation?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune(long consensusTimestamp)] Given that prune() deletes all ContractResult records up to a given consensusTimestamp without checking senderId or payerAccountId, can an unprivileged user use prune() to erase evidence of their own unauthorized transactions, preventing detection of transactions that lacked proper signer approval?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune(long consensusTimestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the lack of rate limiting or access control on prune() to perform a denial-of-service attack by continuously deleting ContractResult records, preventing legitimate contract owners from verifying their authorized transactions?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune(long consensusTimestamp)] Since prune() uses a JPQL DELETE query with no contractId or payerAccountId filter, can an unprivileged user supply a consensusTimestamp that targets a specific time window to delete only the ContractResult records of a targeted contract owner, effectively revoking their transaction history without authorization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune(long consensusTimestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that prune() returns an int (count of deleted rows) with no caller verification to infer the number of ContractResult records for a specific time range, leaking information about contract activity without owner consent?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with senderId set to a high-value account's EntityId, causing the mirror node to record a contract call as originating from that account without their signature, enabling impersonation of the account owner?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Given that ContractResult.senderId defaults to payerAccountId in processContractResult() without cryptographic verification, can an unprivileged user craft a record item where senderId is overridden via functionResult.hasSenderId() to point to another account, causing the mirror node to record a transaction as authorized by that account?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with a transactionHash that matches a previously pruned legitimate transaction, causing the mirror node to resurrect a deleted transaction record attributed to another account without their re-authorization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Since ContractResult.functionResult stores the raw protobuf bytes of ContractFunctionResult with no signature check, can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with crafted functionResult bytes that encode a different contractId or senderId, causing downstream parsers to attribute the result to an unauthorized account?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with gasConsumed set to zero while gasUsed is non-zero, exploiting the lack of consistency validation to make a contract call appear to have consumed no resources, bypassing gas-based authorization throttling?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Given that ContractResult.toContractTransactionHash() builds a ContractTransactionHash using transactionHash, entityId (contractId), and payerAccountId without verifying the payer's authorization, can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult that generates a false ContractTransactionHash linking an unauthorized transaction to a legitimate contract?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune(long consensusTimestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the @Modifying annotation's clearAutomatically behavior (if enabled) to invalidate the persistence context after prune(), causing subsequent reads to miss recently inserted ContractResult records and making authorized transactions appear non-existent?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune(long consensusTimestamp)] Since prune() is defined in the RetentionRepository interface with @Transactional and no access control, can an unprivileged user invoke it through any exposed service endpoint to delete ContractResult records for contracts they do not own, bypassing the requirement for owner authorization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with a negative or zero consensusTimestamp, exploiting the lack of timestamp validation to create a record that sorts before all legitimate records and potentially interferes with prune() operations on authorized transactions?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Given that ContractResult stores createdContractIds as a List<Long> with no ownership verification, can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult claiming to have created contracts owned by other parties, causing the mirror node to falsely record those contracts as children of an unauthorized transaction?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with a forged transactionNonce that matches a legitimate Ethereum transaction's nonce, causing the mirror node to record a replay of an authorized transaction without the original signer's consent?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Since ContractResult.payerAccountId is stored as an EntityId with no cryptographic binding to the actual transaction signer, can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with payerAccountId pointing to a high-value account, causing the mirror node to record a contract execution as paid for by that account without their authorization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with callResult containing crafted bytes that mimic a successful token transfer return value, causing downstream EVM-compatible consumers to believe an authorized token transfer occurred without the token owner's approval?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune(long consensusTimestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that prune() does not verify whether the records being deleted are referenced by other tables (e.g., contract_log, contract_action) to create orphaned records that reference deleted ContractResult entries, corrupting the integrity of authorized transaction data?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Given that ContractResult has no field for storing the multi-signature threshold or required signers, can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult for a multi-sig contract call that was never approved by the required number of signers, causing the mirror node to record it as a valid authorized execution?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with a contractId matching a system contract (e.g., 0.0.365 hook contract) and a forged senderId, causing the mirror node to record an unauthorized call to a privileged system contract as if it were approved by a legitimate account?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Since ContractResult.bloom is set via setBloom() which replaces EMPTY_BLOOM with an empty byte array but does not validate the bloom content, can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with a crafted bloom that falsely indicates specific event signatures occurred, causing downstream systems to trigger actions based on unauthorized events?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: save(ContractResult)] Can an unprivileged user insert a ContractResult with transactionResult set to a SUCCESS code for a transaction that was actually rejected by the network, causing the mirror node to record an unauthorized transaction as successfully executed without signer approval?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/ContractResultRepository.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune(long consensusTimestamp)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the absence of a minimum consensusTimestamp bound in prune() to delete ContractResult records with very recent timestamps, effectively erasing evidence of just-executed authorized transactions before they can be confirmed by the contract owner?
